Winslow Tandler ← Part II
SECOND ORDER
Issue 07 · July 15, 2026
AI, Capital & Work

How Long Can an AI Chip Earn?

Separating prompt processing from answer generation can give older GPUs another job later in life. The reviewed loans still depend on the original customer’s payments.

Read the companion deck (PDF)→

THE CLAIM

Separating prompt processing from answer generation can give older GPUs productive work. In a February 2026 NVIDIA and Sarvam AI demonstration, separation and related tuning helped lift completed-token throughput from a 1.00 baseline to 2.00 on one H100 system. The reviewed loans take the GPUs as collateral but repay from original customer payments. At a power-bound site, our model puts the B200’s required added-output price at about $0.126 per million tokens using $32,000 per nameplate kW, three years and 70% utilization. DeepSeek’s $0.28 first-party V4 Flash output price is one customer-price anchor. Integration work, downtime, financing cost and the H100’s sale value are omitted, so the comparison does not demonstrate payback. Our base case assigns no earnings to years 6 to 10 without post-contract economics for a named fleet.

01 · TWO PHASES, ONE ANSWER

An AI answer has two different jobs inside it

An AI model does two different jobs when it answers a question. It first processes the prompt and builds a working memory, then produces the answer one small unit of text at a time. The first job wants a burst of computation; the second often waits on memory and communication. Running them on different machines can give an older GPU a useful role alongside a newer or specialized processor, provided the working memory moves between them quickly enough.

02 · THE THROUGHPUT EVIDENCE

The demonstrated gains cluster around 2 to 5 times, with conditions

NVIDIA and Sarvam AI reported in February 2026 that separation and related tuning lifted completed-token throughput on one H100 system from a 1.00 baseline to 2.00 while maintaining 75 output tokens per second per user. The split itself accounted for roughly a 1.5 times step. A June 2026 research prototype paired Tenstorrent hardware with NVIDIA A100s and reported as much as 3.2 times more useful throughput and 4.8 times more useful throughput per dollar. AWS found in July 2026 that the split worked best for long prompts and heavy simultaneous use. Below its 4,096-token routing threshold, moving the working memory cost more time than the separation saved.

03 · THE POWER-BOUND HURDLE

At a full site, the constraint is power, not chip age

The economic hurdle rises when electricity and rack space are already full. MLCommons v5.0 results and NVIDIA specifications imply that a complete B200 server produces about 2.3 times as many completed tokens per maximum input kW as an H100 server. Our model asks whether the B200’s extra output could cover a purchase priced at $32,000 per nameplate kW. Over three years at 70% utilization, it requires about $0.126 per million added tokens. DeepSeek lists V4 Flash output at $0.28, a single first-party customer-price anchor. Realized operator margin can differ from that public price. The comparison also excludes integration work, downtime, financing cost and the H100’s sale value, so it does not establish payback. Cheap power, spare capacity or a differentiated workload can still make retention sensible.

04 · THE DEBT CLOCKS

Five operators cluster at five to six years; only IREN matches all three

Five operators report equipment-life estimates of five to six years and customer terms of three to five years; a broader financing sample puts disclosed GPU debt at two to six years. IREN is the one operator in the sample that aligns the equipment life, Microsoft contract and project debt on the same five-year project. The debt is due no later than Microsoft’s final service payment. CoreWeave’s 2026 loans likewise rely on contracted cash flow, guarantees, reserves and control of identified GPUs. Their six-year contractual depreciation convention supplies no market appraisal.

05 · WHAT WOULD CHANGE THE ANSWER

The next test is a named fleet’s economics after year five

Separating the phases may keep older chips useful after their initial job. Three events would move later years into the lending case: an operator reports post-contract economics, a buyer establishes a used-fleet price, or a lender finances the next workload after the first contract. If those events occur, retirements could slow as each new GPU generation enters service. Whether owners or customers receive the benefit would then depend on how much compute prices fall as supply expands.

THE SECOND-ORDER EFFECT · WHAT CHANGES IF OLDER GPUS DEVELOP A REAL SECOND MARKET

Immediate finding. Separating prompt processing from answer generation can give an A100 or H100 another productive assignment, and CoreWeave reports several GPU generations earning revenue at the same time. Today’s public loan structures protect repayment with contracted cash flow, parent support, reserves and control of the machines.

Broader consequence. If operators could document that later workload, they could keep more older GPUs in service as they add new systems. New GPUs would still enter the best-powered sites, while older high-memory fleets could move to cheaper electricity, less urgent work or facilities that cannot support the newest systems. Financing that transition would require prices for used fleets, independent inspection and valuation, specialist owners willing to take customer turnover risk, and loans for the network, processor or site work needed to redeploy the machines. That infrastructure would also have to spread risk across more names than the current market supplies: the same providers of chips and capital recur across the ten 2025 to 2026 financings we reviewed, with NVIDIA appearing in nine. The share of the benefit going to owners or customers would depend on whether added capacity is absorbed at prior prices or pushes compute prices down.

SELECTED SOURCES AND CAVEATS
  • NVIDIA and Sarvam AI, “How NVIDIA Extreme Hardware-Software Co-Design Delivered a Large Inference Boost for Sarvam AI’s Sovereign Models” (February 18, 2026)
  • AWS Machine Learning Blog, disaggregated prefill and decode on SageMaker HyperPod (July 10, 2026)
  • HMA-Serve research preprint, arXiv:2606.29986 (June 2026)
  • MLCommons Inference v5.0 completed-token results and NVIDIA DGX H100/B200 system specifications
  • CoreWeave 2025 Form 10-K, Q1 2026 earnings-call materials, and DDTL 4.0 and DDTL 5.0 credit agreements
  • Bloomberg, “CoreWeave Raises $8.5 Billion GPU Loan Backed by Meta Deal” (March 31, 2026)
  • Nebius 2025 Form 20-F and Q1 2026 Form 6-K
  • IREN fiscal 2025 Form 10-K and Microsoft project financing Form 8-K
  • WhiteFiber 2025 Form 10-K and Q1 2026 Form 10-Q
  • Applied Digital Cloud transaction information statement
  • DeepSeek V4 Flash pricing
  • Second Order calculations based on the cited technical materials, company filings, and price series

The technical evidence is vendor demonstrations, an SEC-filed announcement, and one research prototype; no operator discloses a post-year-five Hopper or Ampere fleet earning inside a heterogeneous system. Estimates and single-sourced figures carry a visible caveat where they appear. Second Order is an independent research briefing, for discussion only and not investment advice.
